We start off with perception and today We'll focus on what I'll call the building blocks of perception Okay, so we talked about Sensation and perception and what's the distinction between them and it's kind of a fuzzy point But sensation really is going to be talking about how do we take external information whether it's lights or sounds smells touches or whatever how do we actually take that information and Translate it into a neural code because for the brain to process it has to be converted from say light waves Into neural signals, and so that's called transduction There's mechanisms for that for all the different senses whether it's the photo-reception photoreceptors in the eye that have certain pigments that respond to light waves, wavelengths of light, or mechanoreceptors in the fingertips.
We're actually not gonna spend any real time on that, talking about that.
Let's talk about it in the chapter briefly, but here we're more interested towards the cognitive end, so perception.
In perception, you think about perception, but to me, perception really actually gets into memory, because it's about, how do we take our sensory input and recognize it?

And to recognize it means that we must have actually had some sort of act of retrieving information from memory, or at least some comparison of the current sensory input with what we have experienced in the past.
Sometimes there'll be a match, or we'll recognize something. We'll say, you know, that's your phone.

Other times, there'll be something we've never seen before, like a green elephant, but at least by failing to recognize it, by saying it doesn't match anything in memory, we recognize that this is something new that we haven't experienced before.
Now, sensation certainly constrains perception. I wanna make that point.

So, we talk about that there is a spectrum of electromagnetic energy, and if it's a spectrum, it means it can go from, you know, wide wavelengths to very narrow wavelengths, or high frequency, or low frequency would be wide ones to high frequency, things that are oscillating rapidly.
This is actually, let's say, the whole spectrum here, going from, again, high frequency to low frequency, at least in the electromagnetic.
This is different than what we mean with sound pressure waves.
But the point I want to really make here is that our eyes are tuned at, we're only sensitive, our visual system is only sensitive to a very narrow region of that electromagnetic spectrum.
It's really this range right here, and if we see it down at the lowest end where we become sensitive to it, that's where we see blues and purples.
As we go across, we have yellows, and then at this end, we see reds.
So forth, when you're seeing a rainbow, it's basically being that light being refracted so that we're actually highlighting the full spectrum of what's visible to the human eye.
Now, that's human visual sensitivity, but actually, it differs for other species.
So for instance, we don't see ultraviolet light, but some animals do see ultraviolet light.

If we actually were sensitive to ultraviolets and ultraviolet light, this here is color-coding variations in the ultraviolet light across that primrose surface there.

And so now it's got this very high contrast in the ultraviolet range that allows the bee to hone right in, something that we're completely insensitive to.
All right, so with that in mind, we're gonna consider today, briefly, well, talk a little bit about the anatomy of the visual.
We're gonna focus only on the visual system. Other senses are talked about in the textbook, but I, like many others, we're kind of visual-centric.

So we're gonna talk about the anatomy of the visual system, how we can think about the building blocks of perception from an anatomical perspective, from a physiological perspective, and from a behavioral perspective.
So again, the three sorts of, three arms of cognitive neuroscience about how we can understand brain-behavioral relationships.
We're looking at anatomy, physiology, and behavior. All right, so first, anatomy, and this is, again, taken straight from the textbook here.
Just highlighting in color what we call the primary sensory areas, and here I'm showing it for all the senses.

Primary just means the first part in the cerebral cortex where inputs from the sensory systems have reached the cerebral cortex, and they've all gone through a number of different synapses out at the eye, for instance, for the visual system in the retina, and then some projections through the subcortex, and then eventually, usually through the thalamus, reaching the cerebral cortex, and so for visual information, it's gonna be back here in the occipital lobe, and then we have the primary motor, primary somatosensory cortex here, olfactory, et cetera, and I'm showing here, actually, in color both the primary and the secondary, and the secondary would just mean the main relay of information from the primary sensory area to another sensory area, but again, this information's gonna get mixed and matched very quickly, and so other areas of the brain we'll talk about as being, you know, multimodal, and multimodal just means that if we did, say, physiology, we recorded from neurons in those parts of the brain, we'd see that they don't just respond to visual inputs, they might respond to both visual and auditory inputs, right, and they might respond to visual and tactile inputs, and you can see that these, that these multimodal areas tend to lie in these gaps between the primary and secondary regions up here.
Some areas respond to inputs from many different senses, some from just pairs of them, that's the point being made in this slide here.

But again, just turning to a blow-up of the visual system, this is just laying out the basic anatomy, something that we could do by using some sort of projection map that is injecting a stain, say an osoma, and seeing where its axons go if we're doing the anterograde tracing method.
So of course we know the input's going to come in through the eyes, in the back of the eyes all the photoreceptors are along the retina.
And this is just showing, kind of the thickness here is showing the projections of the retina are going to go to many different areas of the subcortex.
I showed you earlier that there's this large projection into the thalamus and in particular what's called the lateral geniculate nucleus of the thalamus.
One thing I want to make clear is, you'll hear the word nucleus described very often when people talk about structures within the brainstem or within the subcortex.

A nucleus can also be, every cell has its own nucleus, but when we talk in anatomical terms about a nucleus, in terms of neuroanatomy, we're usually talking about a cluster of neurons that are in the subcortex that usually have some sort of functional relationship to one another.

So the lateral geniculate nucleus is one cluster of neurons within the thalamus, there's the medial geniculate nucleus, there's the pulvonar, these are all located within the thalamus, they tend to be anatomically clustered together and they have some sort of functionality.
So the word nucleus has this double meaning between what we talk about when we talk about the nucleus of a cell versus when we're talking about some part of the brain.
So again, most of the output from the retina is going up to the lateral geniculate nucleus and then on to the primary visual cortex and from there it goes on to secondary visual areas.
I've drawn the arrows both directions here to say that it's not a one-way flow of information, there's also going to be feedback from the secondary visual areas into the primary visual cortex.

There's also going to be lots of projections from the retina into other subcortical structures and I don't have to worry about them in detail, but the pulvonar is a major structure that seems to be involved in the orienting of attention, the superior colliculus is a subcortical structure that's really important for moving your eyes or directing the eyes about where they're going to be moving as different information comes into the scene.
You know, lots of critters don't have a cortex, but they're going to have something similar to these subcortical structures and they're capable of quite complex vision.
So we don't need to have a cerebral cortex to have complex vision, there's many species that get by with just these subcortical structures here.
And of course there's going to be, it's not ever a one-way flow of information processing in the brain, there's almost always reciprocal projections.
An area that projects to one area is probably going to receive feedback back from that area.
So that's sort of the basic layout of the anatomy.

And if it doesn't get recognized at any point, it hits the hippocampus and that's a great signal that says, hey, nothing's recognized here, this is something new, I have to learn about this.
And so it's the way the visual system can then parlay into the learning system to say, I haven't figured this out, hippocampus, help me recognize what this new stimulus is.

All right, so that's a big complicated picture. Here's kind of the simplified story in the sense of what we do seem to have is based on mainly functionality is that there's two prominent visual pathways.
So lots of different visual areas within each of these, but really we can talk about a ventral pathway and a dorsal pathway.
So ventral means coming down low from the occipital lobe into the temporal lobe.
Dorsal means up above, going from the visual occipital lobe up into the parietal lobe.
Okay, and this is just highlighting occipital cortex, we have V1 all the way through V4 and middle temporal regions.
But then we get into the parietal lobe, we have these regions, we have these.

Say if we were doing primate neurophysiology, and you can see the same thing probably if you did human fMRI work, that if we're at the retina, the optimal stimulus is just a tiny dot of light.
Because right, the brain is always responsive to change, so if everything was a big same color, there's no change out there, there's nothing really to be noticed.
But out here we need to have very small spots of light.
of light because our photoreceptors are tuned to just a tiny part of space.

The better case is the patient who's described as really being terrified to cross the street.

Because she sees the cars, she knows they're moving, she gets these snapshots, but she doesn't have that capability that you all have, which is to take that smooth input from your motion system and be able to extrapolate how far away that car is and when it's going to reach you.
And so motion becomes this inferential process rather than a direct perceptual process for these individuals.
